text_excerpt: News – McConnell International Foundation Skip to content Home Who We Are Menu Toggle Our Vision Our Trustees What We Do Menu Toggle Keep Girls in School Previous Projects Supporting Children Livingstone Volunteers News Fundraising Contact Us Donate Donate Main Menu Home Who We Are Menu Toggle Our Vision Our Trustees What We Do Menu Toggle Keep Girls in School Previous Projects Supporting Children Livingstone Volunteers News Fundraising Contact Us Donate Donate News Our review of Keep Girls in School 2025 Catch up on all our news with our annual review which has just been published. Thanks to all our supporters who made 2025 our most successful year yet. Your generosity, advice and support means that 200 girls completed another year of secondary education – changing their lives for the better. 2025 Annual Report Keep Girls in School 2025: A second chance for girls to help them beat the odds This was a big year for many of the 200 girls that we support through our Keep Girls in School Programme. The 100 students in the Ntichisi and Zomba Rural districts sat their national Junior Certificate of Education (JCE) exams in June, which a girls must pass if she is to progress to Year 3. The results arrived in August, just before the new school year began on September 22 and once again the MIF scholars far exceeded the national average pass rate. Ninety-five girls sat the JCE exams. The results showed that 88 girls passed – 89 per cent of our scholars, compared to a national pass rate for girls of 73.5 per cent. The overall national pass rate was 77.6 per cent). We are delighted to say that the seven girls who failed have been given a second chance to repeat Year 2 and resit the JCE exams next June. This new programme means that girls who fail their JCE exam will not have to leave school at the end of second year. Of the five who did not sit the exams, one girl had moved to another school earlier in the school year and four were absent because they had dropped out. Of the four girls who dropped out of school, one was from Malomo CDSS and three from its neighbouring school Kaulatsitsi CDSS. This is a 4 percent drop-out, compared to the annual average rate of 10 per cent (UNICEF).It is a new school, only opened four years ago, and is situated in in a very conservative rural community where traditional beliefs about women and girls still hold sway. The headteacher Ellen Chinawaexplained that there were two issues at play: secondary education was not yet embedded in the local culture and girls are expected to give up education at the end of primary school as there is no value attached to a secondary education. She explained that when the school opened there were 30 girls in year 1, of that cohort there are now only five in Year 4.Our KGIS scholars are changing these outcomes. The Kaulatsitsi pass rate of 18 girls out of 25 is 72 per cent, very close to the national average and there will be 25 girls in Year 2 and 3 supported by KCA and MIF – the entire year 3 cohort (21 girls) and 4 in Year 2 (repeating). MIF scholars at Kaulatsitsi CDSS, October 2025 Our Malawi partner, WOLREC with support from the Foundation and our partner Keep A Child Alive and working with the headteacher and community leaders will deliver a sensitisation programme for parents and guardians early in 2026, to help them understand the benefits of keeping girls in school. The other cohort of 100 girls are at schools in Balaka and Salima Districts. These girls range from year 1 students to year 4. This year 28 girls sat their national Malawi Certificate of Secondary Education exams (MSCE). with 12 passing. This is a pass rate of 43 per cent compared to the national pass rate for girls of 53 per cent and an overall pass rate of 58 per cent. The girls who failed have all been given the chance to repeat year 4 so that they may resit their MSCE exams in July 2026. Margret (L) with Debora. Two of the students who will repeat Year 4 thanks to the Foundation’s Second Chance to L
